points: Nationalism emerged in nineteenth-century Europe as a political ideology and movement transforming European societies from multi-national dynastic empires to nation-states, where citizens shared collective identity, common history, and sense of belonging.

The French Revolution (1789) marked the first clear expression of nationalism by transferring sovereignty from absolute monarchy to citizens and implementing policies (uniform laws, language, symbols, administration) to create collective national consciousness.

Liberal nationalism of the early nineteenth century combined individual freedom, equality before law, representative government, and national unification ideals, particularly among educated middle classes seeking to challenge aristocratic dominance.

summary.category: Cultural and Ideological Foundations of Nationalism
points: Romanticism—emphasising emotion, intuition, and folk culture over reason—played a crucial role in making nationalism emotionally resonant by celebrating vernacular languages, folk traditions, and national heritage as expressions of the authentic national spirit (Volksgeist).

National symbols and allegories—flags (tricolour), anthems, female figures (Marianne in France, Germania in Germany)—were created and promoted to make abstract nationalism concrete and visually memorable for masses, appearing on coins, stamps, and public monuments.

Language became a powerful tool of national identity and resistance, as seen in Poland where Polish-language instruction became an act of defiance against Russian occupation, and in the Grimm brothers' project of collecting and preserving German folklore against French cultural dominance.

Congress of Vienna (1815): Conservative reaction attempting to suppress revolutionary nationalism and restore traditional monarchies; established German Confederation of 39 states but failed to prevent growing nationalist and liberal sentiments.

Age of Revolutions (1830–1848): Liberal-nationalist uprisings across Europe (July Revolution in France 1830; Greek independence 1821–1829; German and Italian unification movements; Silesian weavers' uprising 1845) demanding constitutions and national unification, though mostly suppressed.

Post-1848 Transformation: Nationalism was appropriated by conservative forces; Germany unified under Prussian military leadership (Three Wars: 1864, 1866, 1870–71; proclamation of German Emperor 1871) and Italy unified through Cavour's diplomacy and Garibaldi's campaigns (1859–1870), both 'from above' rather than through popular revolution.

British nationalism developed uniquely through cultural dominance of England (imposed English language, suppressed Scottish Gaelic and Celtic traditions, incorporated Ireland forcibly into United Kingdom 1801), creating hierarchical union rather than popular nation-state.

Nationalism remained primarily an elite phenomenon; illiterate peasant masses (especially in southern Italy) remained largely unaware of nationalist ideology despite participating in unification movements, revealing the gap between elite nationalist projects and popular consciousness.

Late nineteenth-century nationalism transformed from liberatory ideology to narrow, competitive nationalism linked to imperial domination; in the Balkans, nationalist aspirations of Slavic peoples became entangled with great-power rivalries (Russia, Germany, England, Austria-Hungary), contributing to World War I (1914).

The European nation-state model—developed through nineteenth-century European history—became globally accepted as the 'natural' and universal form of political organisation, exported worldwide through imperialism.

Anti-colonial and anti-imperial movements in twentieth-century colonised countries adopted nationalism as their ideology, though developing their own specific varieties reflecting local histories, demonstrating nationalism's adaptability and global reach beyond its European origins.
